resume:
Greg Halloran — VP Operations

12+ years in operations leadership across e-commerce fulfillment and last-mile delivery.

- VP Operations, regional e-commerce fulfillment (2019-present): own 3 distribution centers, ~900 staff, P&L for fulfillment cost.
- Director of Ops, 3PL provider (2015-2019): ran client onboarding and warehouse productivity programs.
- Ops Manager, retail DC (2011-2015).

Focus areas: warehouse throughput, labor planning, cost-per-unit, SLA/on-time performance, safety.
Education: B.S. Industrial Engineering. Lean Six Sigma Black Belt.
jd: High-growth retailer seeks a VP of Operations to own a multi-site fulfillment network (4 DCs, ~1,200 associates) reporting to the COO. You will own cost-per-unit-shipped, on-time-ship SLA, peak-season readiness, and a safety record under heavy scrutiny. The immediate mandate: take 12% out of fulfillment cost-per-unit over 18 months without breaking the 98% on-time SLA, while integrating a new WMS and absorbing 30% volume growth. We need an operator who can be specific about the levers — labor model, slotting, automation ROI, carrier mix — and who can tell a crisp before/after story with numbers. You will present peak readiness and cost programs to the executive team. Calm under operational fire is essential.

history.q: Give me the shape of your current role — sites, headcount, the cost number you own.
What's your current cost-per-unit-shipped, and where was it when you started?
Pick the single biggest lever you pulled on cost. Walk me through it.
When you changed the labor model, what specifically changed — shifts, flex staffing, productivity standards?
How did you protect on-time SLA while taking out cost?
Tell me about a peak season that went sideways. What happened?
You've done a WMS-adjacent change before — how did you de-risk the cutover?
Talk about your safety record. Any serious incident, and what you changed after?
How do you decide whether an automation investment pays back?
Carrier mix — give me a decision where you shifted volume between carriers and why.
Let's get concrete: that biggest cost lever — what was the cost-per-unit before and after, and over how many months?

candidate_last_answer: Right, so, okay — the cost-per-unit thing, that's, um, that's a great question. So there were a lot of moving parts, honestly, and it's funny because peak that year was insane, we had a whole thing with one of the carriers, but anyway. So the labor model — we did a bunch there, and slotting, slotting was huge, I keep meaning to pull the exact slide. Before and after... I want to say it came down meaningfully, like it was a good chunk, I'd have to check the deck for the exact number but it was real, the CFO was happy. Over how many months — it was kind of a rolling thing, you know, some of it landed fast and some of it took a while, and then peak kind of muddied the numbers for a quarter. Sorry, what was the second part of your question?

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: like it was a good chunk, I'd have to check the deck for the exact number

expected-yield: cut through the rambling and force a single committed number — the before and after cost-per-unit (even a best-estimate range) and the specific number of months, plus which lever (labor vs slotting) actually drove most of the delta, so the vague 『good chunk』 becomes a verifiable figure
